coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescein modified carbon quantum dots and application of dual emission ratio fluorescent probes for highly selective detection of chlorothalonil
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qing
Wang, Jianghua
Zhang, Zikang
Xu, Yiluo
Fu, Yingqiang
coredata.dc:description: Chlorothalonil (CHT) is a class 2B carcinogen that is capable of remaining in the environment for long periods, has an obvious bioconcentration phenomenon, and may pollute groundwater, thus it is essential to construct a simple detection method for chlorothalonil. However, common single emission fluorescent probes or dual emission fluorescent probes constructed from multi-component mixtures have low selectivity and anti-interference ability. In this work, a simple two-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize fluorescein-modified carbon quantum dots (F-CQDs), and a dual-emission fluorescent probe was constructed to detect chlorothalonil concentration. The study found that in the presence of a certain amount of Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of F-CQDs located at 435 nm and 508 nm gradually decreased with the increase of CHT concentration within 2 min. This fluorescent probe has specific recognition for CHT and shows a significant linear relationship in the range of 0.1664–3.0695 μM, with a detection limit as low as 8.6 × 10−3 μM. The probe was successfully applied to the detection of CHT residual content in environmental water with a satisfactory average recovery, and the present study provides a new method for the preparation of dual-emission fluorescent probes and the detection of organic pesticides and shows great potential in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescein-modified carbon quantum dots
Chlorothalonil
Ratiometric fluorescent probes

Detection of chlorothalonil in real environmental water samples
UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of F-CQDs
XPS and XRD characterization of F-CQDs
Optimization of synthesis conditions and stability of F-CQDs
Mechanism of F-CQD detection of CHT
Detection of residual CHT in actual water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy image of CQDs; (b) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of CQDs; (c) particle size distribution of CQDs; (d) transmission electron microscopy image of F-CQDs; (e) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of F-CQDs; (f) particle size distribution of F-CQDs.
1HNMR spectra of CQDs, F-CQDs, and fluorescein.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, (b) UV–visible absorption, excitation, and emission fluorescence spectra of F-CQDs.
(a) XPS full-spectrum scans of F-CQDs; (b) high-resolution and fitted C1s spectra; (c) high-resolution and fitted N1s spectra; (d) high-resolution and fitted O1s spectra.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-CQDs@Fe2+ solution after addition of different concentrations of CHT; (b) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F-CQDs@Fe2+ at 435 nm and 508 nm is linearly related to the concentration of CHT.
(a) Effect of different pesticides on the fluorescence performance of F-CQDs@Fe2+, (b) F-CQDs@Fe2+ measurement probe in the presence of other molecules and ions for anti-interference performance.

(a) The fluorescence lifetimes of the F-CQDs, F-CQDs@Fe2+ and F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT systems at 435 nm; (b) FT-IR spectra of F-CQDs, F-CQDs@CHT and F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT; (c) the fluorescence lifetimes of the F-CQDs, F-CQDs@Fe2+ and F −CQDs@Fe2+@CHT systems at 508 nm (d) Zeta potentials of F-CQDs, F-CQDs@Fe2+ and F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT.
Detection of CHT in water samples by using F-CQDs@Fe2+ as a fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescein modified carbon quantum dots and application of dual emission ratio fluorescent probes for highly selective detection of chlorothalonil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorothalonil (CHT) is a class 2B carcinogen that is capable of remaining in the environment for long periods, has an obvious bioconcentration phenomenon, and may pollute groundwater, thus it is essential to construct a simple detection method for chlorothalonil. However, common single emission fluorescent probes or dual emission fluorescent probes constructed from multi-component mixtures have low selectivity and anti-interference ability. In this work, a simple two-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize fluorescein-modified carbon quantum dots (F-CQDs), and a dual-emission fluorescent probe was constructed to detect chlorothalonil concentration. The study found that in the presence of a certain amount of Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of F-CQDs located at 435 nm and 508 nm gradually decreased with the increase of CHT concentration within 2 min. This fluorescent probe has specific recognition for CHT and shows a significant linear relationship in the range of 0.1664–3.0695 μM, with a detection limit as low as 8.6 × 10−3 μM. The probe was successfully applied to the detection of CHT residual content in environmental water with a satisfactory average recovery, and the present study provides a new method for the preparation of dual-emission fluorescent probes and the detection of organic pesticides and shows great potential in environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlorothalonil (CHT), a broad-spectrum organochlorine fungicide, is widely used in agriculture to control fungal diseases on vegetables, trees, and other crops [1]. However, its impact on environmental media such as water and soil in the environment has become a focus of environmental science research [2]. It was shown that chlorothalonil is not only present in its original form in these media but also produces a variety of metabolites such as 4-hydroxychlorothalonil, 2,5,6-trichloro-4-methoxyisophthalonitrile and m-phenylene Di carbonitrile [3]. These metabolites may be more toxic and environmentally persistent than chlorothalonil itself, especially in soil, where concentrations of 4-hydroxychlorothalonil can reach 0.7 μg/g, suggesting potential for accumulation and transport in the environment. Residues of chlorothalonil in the environment are of global concern due to its low degradation rate and high cumulative toxicity [4]. The European Commission has set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for chlorothalonil for different agricultural products to protect food safety and public health [2]. In particular, the overuse of pesticides not only poses a threat to the environment but may also be toxic to crop plants and affect food safety. The limited penetration of ultraviolet radiation may prolong the residual time of pesticides in plant products, making it difficult to completely remove pesticide residues that have penetrated the internal tissues by conventional washing and soaking methods. Consequently, the reduction of pesticide residues, especially chlorothalonil, is a key issue to be addressed in the field of environmental sciences [5,6]. This is not only related to food safety and environmental protection, but also an important component of achieving sustainable agriculture. Studying the concentrations of chlorothalonil and its metabolites related to the environment in aquatic systems, and evaluating their biotransformation and potential risks, is crucial for developing effective risk management strategies and protecting the environment and human health. [7–9].

Conventional methods for chlorothalonil detection, such as chromatography [10], mass spectrometry [11], and electrochemical methods [12,13] e.g., chromatography and mass spectrometry require complex pre-treatment steps, expensive instruments, and the use of a large number of organic solvents, which can increase the cost of the detection and cause environmental pollution. Despite the high sensitivity of electrochemical methods, the stability of the results is easily affected by the electrode properties, and the performance is sensitive to environmental conditions and easily affected by temperature and humidity [14]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop new analytical techniques that are simple, fast, and economical [15–18].

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), an emerging class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have received a lot of attention in recent years in both scientific and industrial domains [19,20]. CQDs have many excellent properties, such as tiny size, excellent fluorescence performance, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, which make them play a crucial role in many fields, particularly for environmental monitoring and treatment [21–23]. However, single nano sensing materials are often more susceptible to interference from coexisting substances when analyzing target analytes, thereby limiting their selectivity and anti-interference ability. Post-modification of nanomaterials to form composites can effectively improve the selectivity and anti-interference ability of detection [24,25], e.g., Farhad Salimi1 et al. reported a colorimetric sensor based on ribavirin-modified gold nanoparticles (ribavirin-AuNPs) for the detection of Cr3+ and Ni2+ in aqueous solutions ions showed good selectivity and sensitivity [26]. Elahe Ahmadi et al. reported a carboxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (c-MWCNTs) and 11-mercaptoundecanoylhydrazine carbamide-coated silver nanoparticles (MUHC-AgNPs) modified for the detection of the antiretroviral drug nevirapine (NVP) also achieved good results [27]. Modified carbon nanomaterials can significantly enhance their properties and expand their application areas through chemical modification, doping, and surface functionalization.

Previously, there were few reports on the use of modified Carbon quantum dots prepared as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of chlorothalonil residues in environmental water samples. The advantages of ratiometric fluorescent probes are that they can determine the concentration of a target analyte by measuring the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths and that they are capable of providing an internal reference, compensating for environmental variations (e.g., pH, temperature, viscosity), and possessing the self-correcting, thus reducing the influence of changes in experimental conditions on the measurement results and improving the reliability of the measurement results [28–30]. The previously reported ratio fluorescent probes mostly consist of a mixed system of several fluorescent materials, and there are few reports on a single material as a ratio fluorescent probe that has both quantum dots properties and dye fluorescence luminescence properties.

In this work, F-CQDs were synthesized by a simple two-step hydrothermal method. This material has two fluorescence emission peaks, one with carbon quantum dots characteristics and the other with small molecule dye-like characteristics. The F-CQDs material aqueous solution can achieve specific recognition and high-sensitivity detection of chlorothalonil in the presence of a certain amount of Fe2+. In addition, fluorescent probes have better selectivity and anti-interference ability towards chlorothalonil in environments with different pesticides and complex ions, enabling real-time monitoring of chlorothalonil residues in the environment.

The anhydrous citric acid (CA), formamide, N, N-dimethylformamide, fluorescein, polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mn = 400), chlorothalonil (CHT), FeCl2, all of these chemical medications were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and are analytically pure. All chemicals used have not undergone any further purification.

The solution ultrasound was performed on a KH-300DE ultrasonic cleaning machine (Kebiao, Wuhu, China), and the solution was centrifuged on a TG16MW desktop high-speed centrifuge (Hexi, Hunan, China). The YW-10A-60 vacuum freeze dryer (Yanwo, Nanjing, China) was used to prepare the solid, and the solution was stirred evenly on an S82-1 magnetic stirrer (Zhiwei, Shanghai, China). The crystal structure was measured using a JEM-2100F (Joel, Japan) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), ESCLAB Xi plus X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to characterize the surface elements and their chemical states of carbon quantum dots, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet Is5, United Kingdom) is used to test the functional groups of the substance, Shimadzu X-ray diffractometer 6100 (XRD) is used to detect the crystal structure of the substance, UV visible spectrophotometer (UV) is performed at UV5500A (Unico, China), fluorescence spectrometer (FL) is performed at Max-4 fluorescence spectrometer (JOBIN YVON technology), and transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLT) (HORIBA, Fluomax-4, Japan) is used to test the fluorescence lifetime of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and post-modification of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescein-modified carbon quantum dots (F-CQDs) were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1.0014 g of anhydrous citric acid, 3 mL of formamide, and 10 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide were thoroughly mixed by ultrasound and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor at 160 °C for 4 h to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for later use. After cooling to ambient temperature, the reaction product and then 0.0527 g of fluorescein and 3 mL of polyethylene glycol (PEG400) were added. After ultrasonication for 10 min, the reaction was continued for 4 h at 160 °C. The cooled solution was centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 15 min, and the supernatant was purified for 48 h using a 500 DA dialysis bag. After rotary evaporating the obtained solution for 6 h using a rotary evaporator, a concentrated solution of F-CQDs was obtained. The concentrated solution was then freeze-dried in a vacuum freeze dryer for 12 h. Ultimately, the resulting solid powder was kept for subsequent use at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The determination of CHT is carried out according to the following steps: prepare CHT standard solution (10−4 mg/L) using ethanol and ultrapure water in a ratio of 2:8 as solvents, take 250 μL of F-CQDs solution and 200 μL FeCl2 (10−3mol/L), add deionized water to 3 mL, react for 2 min, and then add different concentrations of CHT standard solution in sequence. Fluorescence intensity was measured under excitation at 340 nm, and the fluorescence emission peaks of F-CQDs were quenched to varying degrees at 435 nm and 508 nm. Every experiment was carried out three times at room temperature.

To evaluate the detection system's selectivity, common coexisting compounds with concentrations of 10−4mol/L were added to F-CQDs, including Diptera (DIP), bifenthrin (BIF), chlorpheniramine (CHL), Phosphoethyl Al (EAP), fipronil (FIP), folpet (FOL), triadimefon (TRI), 2,4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid (DA), and acetamiprid (ACE).

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the detection system, a substance with a concentration of 10−4 mol/L was added to the F-CQDs fluorescence system in the presence of a mixed solution of CHT and Fe2+. Common ions include Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Ni2+, Ag+, NO2 –, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2–, NO3 –.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of chlorothalonil in real environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water sample was taken from the Yangtze River water in Anhui (China). The lake water is collected from Fengxing Lake and Mengxi Lake at the main campus of Anhui Polytechnic University. The water samples have been purified using a 0.45 μm microporous filter, centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate residues, and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for future use. 1.0 mL of distilled water was accurately pipetted to 3 mL of the pretreated sample, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at the excitation wavelength parameter of 340 nm. To verify the method's reliability, a spiked recovery experiment was designed: Fe2+ solution and CHT standard were quantitatively added to the original water samples, three concentration gradients of 10, 20, and 30 μM were set, and three parallel experiments were conducted for each concentration. All operations were performed in a standard environmentally controlled laboratory (25 ± 1 °C, 45 ± 5 % relative humidity).

The HR-TEM characterization of the synthesized CQDs and the F-CQDs was performed to obtain their morphology and size distribution. The results are shown in Fig. 1 . From Fig. 1(a) and (d), it can be found that both CQDs and F-CQDs are evenly distributed with good spherical dispersion. Comparing the particle size distribution, the average particle size of CQDs is about 2.85 nm (Fig. 1(c)), while the average particle size of F-CQDs is about 3.25 nm (Fig. 1(f)). The particle size of the F-CQDs increased significantly. In the HR-TEM image shown in Fig. 1(b) (e), the lattice fringe in the core region of the CQDs and the F-CQDs can be observed, and the measured spacing between the lattice fringe is 0.218 nm and 0.184 nm, respectively. The lattice spacing of CQDs is consistent with the data in the literature [31], and the lattice spacing of F-CQDs has changed significantly, indicating that the modification of fluorescein changes the structure of carbon quantum dots.

To further verify that the fluorescein-modified CQDs is a new carbon nanocomposite, 1HNMR analysis of CQDs, fluorescein, and F-CQDs was performed separately, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . Comparing the relative intensity of 1HNMR chemical shifts at 3.5 and 2.5 between CQDs and F-CQDs, it can be seen that the peak at 3.5 in the chemical shift of F-CQDs is significantly enhanced and widened, and the chemical shift slightly shifts towards higher fields. This is consistent with the broadening phenomenon of the 1HNMR peak of organic groups on solid surfaces due to relaxation limitation.

The results of characterizing CQDs and F-CQDs using FT-IR spectroscopy are shown in Fig. 3 . Comparison of the FT-IR spectra of CQDs and F-CQDs shows that F-CQDs which are modified with fluorescein exhibit strong new peaks at 1100 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1. This corresponds to the absorption peaks of the N–H and C–O groups, respectively. This indicates that carbon quantum dots composite materials modified with fluorescein have been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of F-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV visible absorption spectra of F-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 (b), and clear absorption peaks can be observed at 333, 400, and 558 nm, which may correspond to π → π* and n → π* transitions, respectively. The first two absorption peaks correspond to the characteristics of carbon quantum dots, while the latter absorption peaks retain the characteristics of dye fluorescein. Fig. 3(b) also shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of F-CQDs, Fig. 3(b) shows that the fluorescence spectrum when the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelengths are 435 nm and 508 nm. To better understand the properties of the F-CQDs, the fluorescence excitation wavelength dependence of F-CQDs was studied. As shown in Fig. S1, when the excitation wavelength changes from 305 nm to 355 nm, the fluorescence emission peak at 435 nm of F-CQDs shows a significant redshift, while the fluorescence emission peak at 508 nm remains unchanged. Indicating that F-CQDs are new materials of dye-modified carbon quantum dots. In addition, as shown in Fig. S2, when F-CQDs were irradiated with ultraviolet light for 100 h, the fluorescence intensity ratio of F-CQDs remained almost unchanged, indicating that F-CQDs have excellent optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and XRD characterization of F-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface chemical properties of the F-CQDs were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to understand the surface elemental composition and valence states of F-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , Fig. 4(a) shows the full spectrum of F-CQDs. It can be observed from the figure that F-CQDs are mainly composed of C1s, N1s, and O1s. Fig. 4(b) shows the high-resolution C1s spectrum, from which there are CO (288.42 eV), C–N (286.25 eV), and C–O (284.79 eV) in F-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 4(c) shows two peak positions of F-CQDs at 398.06 eV and 399.65 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectrum, corresponding to C–H, and C–N. Fig. 4(d) shows the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, with two binding energy peaks corresponding to CO and C–OH at 530.58 eV and 532.08 eV, respectively. These results indicate the successful synthesis of F-CQDs.

XRD analysis is used to characterize the phase and crystal structure of F-CQDs, as shown in Fig. S3. It can be seen from the figure that a diffraction peak appears at 2θ = 21.94°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of carbon-based nanomaterials, indicating the presence of graphite structure in F-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions and stability of F-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis conditions of F-CQDs were optimized through experiments, and the results are shown in Figs. S4 and S5. The final synthesis time of F-CQDs was determined to be 8 h and the synthesis temperature was 160 °C. Further exploration of the application potential of F-CQDs was conducted, and the effects of pH value, NaCl concentration, and storage time on the stability of F-CQDs fluorescence intensity were evaluated. From the results in Fig. S6, it can be observed that using a series of buffer solutions with pH values ranging from 4.0 to 10.0 as solvents to prepare F-CQDs solutions of the same concentration, the two fluorescence emission peaks of F-CQDs do not show significant changes. This indicates that the F-CQDs composite material can be used within a wide pH range. Adjust the concentration of NaCl to investigate the effect of different salt concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of F-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S7, the results indicate that the effect of ion strength on the fluorescence intensity of F-CQDs can be almost negligible within the range of NaCl concentration from 0-3 mol/L. Further evaluation of the stability of synthesized F-CQDs was conducted, and the fluorescence intensity of F-CQDs under different storage times was studied. The results (Fig. S8) showed that the fluorescence of F-CQDs was relatively stable.

Under the condition of pH 7.0 in the buffer solution, Fe2+ solutions of different concentrations (0–3.22 μM) were added to the F-CQDs solution and allowed to react for 2 min, as shown in Fig. S9 F-CQDs@Fe2 + The fluorescence intensity did not change significantly. Similarly, as shown in Fig. S10, adding CHT solutions of different concentrations (0–3.22 μM) resulted in almost no change in fluorescence intensity. Add 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L Fe2+ 200 μL to the F-CQDs solution and sonicate for 15 min. After 2 min of reaction, add different concentrations of CHT. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), with the increase of CHT concentration, the fluorescence intensity peaks at 435 nm and 508 nm both decrease. The concentration of CHT shows a good linear relationship with F
435
/F
508 , as shown in Fig. 5(b). The linear equations are:
(1)
F 435
F 508
= 0.57396 + 0.08166
C
CHT
Among them, F
435 and F
508 represent the fluorescence intensity of the system at 435 and 508 nm, F
0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample, C
CHT is the concentration of CHT, and the limit of detection (LOD) formula was used to calculate a LOD of 8.6 × 10−3 μM for the linearity of detection of CHT. The results indicate that the ratio fluorescent probe has higher sensitivity. Table S1 shows that this method is relatively satisfactory compared to the reported CHT detection results in the literature.

Different organic pesticides (TCF, BIF, CHL, FAL, FIP, FOL, TRI, FER, ACE) were introduced into the detection system to investigate F-CQDs@Fe2+ Selectivity in CHT detection. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), after adding CHT, the fluorescence intensity ratio of F
435 and F
508 both changed significantly F-CQDs@Fe2+ Fluorescent probes have no significant effect on other organic pesticides, indicating F-CQDs@Fe2+ has good selectivity for CHT. In addition, common interfering ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Na+, NO2 –, K+, Cl−, NH4 +, SO4 2−, Ca2+, CO3 2–, Ni2+, Ag+, NO3 –) were added to the CHT detection system to evaluate its anti-interference ability. In Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensity ratios of F
435 /F
0 and F
508 /F
0 are both close to 1, indicating that F-CQDs@Fe2+ fluorescent sensors have good anti-interference ability in complex environments, which makes them a high potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of F-CQD detection of CHT
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence lifetimes of F-CQDs, F-CQDs@Fe2+, and F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT systems were characterized using a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLT) to investigate the mechanism of F-CQDs@Fe2+ in detecting CHT. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), at the fluorescence emission peak of 435 nm, the fluorescence lifetime of F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT changes from 4.76 ns to 4.82 ns compared to F-CQDs@Fe2+. In Fig. 7(c), the fluorescence lifetime at the emission peak of 508 nm changes from 4.76 ns to 4.37 ns. There is no significant change in a lifetime, indicating that the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT system is not caused by dynamic quenching. That is, the addition of CHT causes a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to static quenching (SQE). The static quenching mechanism is consistent with the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
C q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
C q
where F
0 is the fluorescence intensity without the quenching agent, F is the fluorescence intensity with the quenching agent, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, k
q is the bimolecular quenching rate constant, τ
0 is the fluorescence lifetime without the quenching agent, and C
q is the concentration of the quenching agent. At the fluorescence emission peaks at 435 nm and 508 nm, the fluorescence lifetime before and after ratios τ
0
/τ ≈ 1 when the F-CQDs@Fe2+ fluorescent probe was added with CHT, which is consistent with the characteristics of static quenching.

From the zeta potential characterization results in Fig. 7(d), it can be observed that F-CQDs F-CQDs@Fe2+ and F-CQDs@Fe2+ @CHT are all negative potentials, indicating that the nanoparticles in the system carry negative charges, causing them to repel each other, which helps to maintain the stability of the F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT system. Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of F-CQDs, F-CQDs@CHT, and F-CQDs@Fe2+@CHT (Fig. 7b) can found that the absorption peak at 3400 cm−1 does not change significantly when directly added CHT to the system, while the absorption peak near 3400 cm−1 significantly widens when adding Fe2+ and CHT successively, indicating that the N–H or –OH on the F-CQDs’ surface form hydrogen bonds or coordination bonds with CHT in the presence of Fe2+. This effect alters the electron cloud distribution of surface functional groups of F-CQDs, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the system, further confirming that the mechanism of CHT detection in this system is static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of residual CHT in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Follow the steps described in Section 2.5 to detect the concentration of CHT in actual water samples and add a certain amount of CHT standard solution to test the recovery of the method. As shown in Table 1 , satisfactory recovery was achieved in both the Yangtze River water sample and the lake water sample. The recovery of water samples from the Yangtze River ranges from 99.3 % to 100.4 %, with a relative standard deviation (n = 3) of 0.26 % to 0.41 %. For lake water samples, the recovery ranges from 99.1 % to 100.6 %, with a relative standard deviation (n = 3) of 0.36 % to 0.88 %. This indicates that the F-CQDs@Fe2+ mixed system has strong application prospects as a fluorescent probe for detecting CHT.

In summary, this work synthesized a carbon quantum dots composite material with dual fluorescence emission peaks using a two-step hydrothermal method. The F-CQDs exhibit both carbon quantum dots fluorescence emission and the fluorescence emission peak of dye fluorescein, which are located at 435 nm and 508 nm, respectively. Research has also found that the pesticide CHT cannot quench the fluorescence of F-CQDs, but after adding a certain amount of Fe2+ to F-CQDs solution within 2 min, CHT can quench the two fluorescence emission peaks of F-CQDs solution, and the fluorescence emission peaks at 435 nm and 508 nm are linearly quenched with the concentration of added CHT. A ratio fluorescence probe method was established based on this to detect CHT. The selectivity and anti-interference experiments show that this method has high selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, and better detection sensitivity and minimum detection limit than most CHT detection methods reported in the literature. This also reflects the advantages of fluorescein modified carbon quantum dots. Through the standard addition and recovery experiment of actual water samples, it was found that this method has good performance in detecting CHT residues in environmental water samples and is expected to become an efficient and sensitive fluorescent probe detection method for pesticide CHT residues.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21406001), the National College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310363068).

ce:section.ce:para: Ethics approval was not required for this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126278.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
